coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic study of carbon dots/ Nickel iron layered double hydroxide composite for oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline medium
coredata.dc:creator: Chandrasekaran, Pitchai
Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison, Thomas
Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman, Mathur
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis and strategic deployment of inexpensive, efficient, and robust catalysts for the oxygen evolution reactions (OER) has assumed importance due to their key role in energy conversion and storage. In this work, Cyamopsis tetragonoloba extract derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) supported NiFe layered double hydroxide stacked sheets (NiFe LDH@NDCDs) had been successfully synthesized by easy solvothermal method. Structural morphology and crystalline nature of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs were characterized by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction spectroscopy. The performance of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs in electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) was estimated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel, Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry, and chronopotentiometry measurements in 0.1 M KOH. The synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs demonstrated the small overpotential of 0.385 VRHE to attain current density of 10 mA/cm2, which was lesser than NiFe LDH (0.425 VRHE), the state of the art (RuO2 = 0.422 VRHE), NDCDs (0.534 VRHE) and Bare Ni foam (0.541 VRHE). The calculated Tafel slopes of synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs showed smaller value (93 mV/dec) than NiFe LDH, RuO2, and Bare Ni foam. The EIS studies exposed that the total resistance (R1 + R2) of NiFe LDH@NDCDs (63.85 Ω/cm2) was lower than the total resistance of NiFe LDH (82.58 Ω/cm2) and resistance of Bare Ni foam (123.8 Ω/cm2), which revealed the good conductivity and electro activity of NiFe LDH@NDCDs. Moreover, the stability of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs was analyzed by chronopotentiometry for 6 h at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm2. Thus the results of the present study established NiFe LDH@NDCDs as an efficient electrocatalyst for the OER in alkaline medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen evolution reaction
Cyamopsis tetragonoloba
Layered double hydroxides
NiFe LDH@NDCDs
Electrocatalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs)
Linear sweep voltammetry and Tafel measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) for the financial support for this work through the NET-JRF Award (Award No: 09/715(0020)/2019-EMR-I dated on 02/07/2019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The HR-TEM image of synthesized NDCDs, b) Particle size graph, c) SAED pattern for the NDCDs.
(a,b) SEM images, c) EDX spectrum and (d-f) HR-TEM images of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs.
The XRD pattern for the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs with standard card (inset XRD for NDCDs).
XPS spectra for the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, Deconvolution spectra of the a) C1s level, b) N1s level, c) O1s level, d) Ni2p level, and e) Fe2p level.
The FT-IR spectra of the prepared NDCDs and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs.
a) LSV spectra for the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, NiFe-LDH, RuO2, NDCDs, and Bare Ni foam and b) Bar graph for the corresponding Tafel slope and overpotential values at 10 mA/cm2 (η10).
The Tafel slope for the Bare Ni foam, NDCDs/NF, NiFe-LDH/NF, RuO2/NF, and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF.
Chronopotentiometric analysis of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs for 6 h at 10 mA/cm2.
Nyquist plots for the Bare Ni foam, synthesized NiFe LDH and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF (inset).
a) Evaluation of Electrochemical active surface area by cyclic voltammetry in 0.1 M KOH and (b) linear fit of current density vs scan rate for NiFe-LDH@NDCDs.
Schematic diagram of the behavior of NDCDs for the electrocatalytic OER in the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs composite.

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic study of carbon dots/ Nickel iron layered double hydroxide composite for oxygen evolution reaction in alkaline medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, Dindigul, TN 624 302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, Dindigul, TN 624 302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis and strategic deployment of inexpensive, efficient, and robust catalysts for the oxygen evolution reactions (OER) has assumed importance due to their key role in energy conversion and storage. In this work, Cyamopsis tetragonoloba extract derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) supported NiFe layered double hydroxide stacked sheets (NiFe LDH@NDCDs) had been successfully synthesized by easy solvothermal method. Structural morphology and crystalline nature of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs were characterized by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and X-ray diffraction spectroscopy. The performance of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs in electrocatalytic oxygen evolution reaction (OER) was estimated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel, Electrochemical Impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry, and chronopotentiometry measurements in 0.1 M KOH. The synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs demonstrated the small overpotential of 0.385 VRHE to attain current density of 10 mA/cm2, which was lesser than NiFe LDH (0.425 VRHE), the state of the art (RuO2 = 0.422 VRHE), NDCDs (0.534 VRHE) and Bare Ni foam (0.541 VRHE). The calculated Tafel slopes of synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs showed smaller value (93 mV/dec) than NiFe LDH, RuO2, and Bare Ni foam. The EIS studies exposed that the total resistance (R1 + R2) of NiFe LDH@NDCDs (63.85 Ω/cm2) was lower than the total resistance of NiFe LDH (82.58 Ω/cm2) and resistance of Bare Ni foam (123.8 Ω/cm2), which revealed the good conductivity and electro activity of NiFe LDH@NDCDs. Moreover, the stability of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs was analyzed by chronopotentiometry for 6 h at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm2. Thus the results of the present study established NiFe LDH@NDCDs as an efficient electrocatalyst for the OER in alkaline medium.

ce:section.ce:para:
The need for energy sources has increased the interest exponentially in the branch of energy production and conversion [1]. Alkaline water electrolysis is a significant method for clean and renewable energy conversion technologies, containing rechargeable metal-air batteries and water splitting. In the water electrolysis, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) occurs on the cathode through two-electron transfer reaction, whereas the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) occurs on the anode through 4e- transfer reaction (Eqs. (1) & (2)) [2]. The sluggish kinetics of OER, hugely hampers the HER at the cathode and rises the overpotenial [3].
(1) 2H2O→4H++O2+4e-(Acid)
(2) 4OH-→2H2O+O2+4e-(Base)

In order to reach the lower overpotential, highly performing and active OER catalysts like RuO2 and IrO2 are used [4]. But the usage of noble metals like Ru and Ir in large scale is non-viable due to the shortage and expensive cost. Therefore, the Scientists explore constantly to develop the cost-effective and earth-abundant OER catalysts to accelerate the reactions [5]. Recently, researchers have focused on the transition metals like Ni, Fe, Co, and their hydroxides, oxides, sulfides, borides and selenides because of their excellent electrocatalytic activity towards OER [6–8]. Other than Electrocatalytic water splitting reaction, Metal-organic frameworks (MOF) based photo catalysts also have much attention in the field of water splitting reaction [9]. However, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) draw much attention among the researchers because of their structural adjustability and expectant catalytic activity [10]. Among these LDHs, the low OER overpotential of NiFe-LDH has triggered a greater research attraction due to its ability to refashion the reaction mechanism, because of its tunable layered structure with brucite-like layers. This increases the diffusion rate of reactants and products leading to a faster proton-coupled e- transfer pathway in the water oxidation reaction. However the poor conductivity inhibits their extensive application in electrocatalysis [11,12]. Zhao S et al. reported the formation and utility of composites of carbon associated materials for instance carbon nanodots and graphene with LDHs for water oxidation applications [13,53]. Tianrong Z et al. also described that the incorporation of NiFe-LDH with graphene could boost the electrical conductivity, but fail to enhance the intrinsic OER performance [14]. In order to overcome this failure the nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) supported NiFe-LDH was developed as an alternative electrocatalyst for OER. The nitrogen content in NDCDs is responsible for the larger density of exposed active sites and improvement of the catalytic activity of NDCDs, as the neighbouring carbon atoms are converted into active sites. Particularly, nitrogen containing moieties could combine with protons and favor the formation of carbonaceous composites with myriad active sites. This not only enhances the catalytic properties meritoriously but also hampers the corrosion of catalyst affording good stability [15–17].

CDs are a new form of carbon-based material with a zero-dimensional structure and a size of less than 10 nm having high biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent photostability and electro-catalytic efficiency [18,19]. Due to their larger active sites, they can easily form the composite with NiFe-LDH and enhance the intrinsic catalytic activity. Until now, preparing a nanomaterial using non-toxic, renewable materials and eco-friendly solvents is a major challenge. In this work, simple hydrothermal carbonization method was used in synthesizing the nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) [20].

In this present study, Cyamopsis tetragonoloba (cluster bean or guar bean) belonging to the family Fabaceae has been identified as a green source for synthesizing CDs. The green pods of cluster beans have been reported to contain ascorbic acid (vitamin C), riboflavin, thiamine, niacin, carotene and folic acid in high concentrations. While ß-carotene, and vitamin E are present in low concentrations and iron and protein are present in medium concentrations. Gallic acid is abundant in cluster bean seeds, carbohydrates, calcium, methionine, enzymes like exo-ß-D-mannanase and α-D-galactosidase, polyphenolics like kaempferol-7-glucoside, kaempferol-3-rutinoside, gallotannins, gallic, chlorogenic, caffeic and ellagic acids and fatty acids like palmitic, stearic, oleic, arachidic, linoleic and linolenic acid [21–23].

Herein, facile hydrothermal carbonization process is engaged to synthesize nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) by using cluster beans and aq. ammonia as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to explore the optical properties of NDCDs. Then the synthesized NDCDs are blended with NiFe-LDH using a simple hydrothermal method. The main advantage of this method is to enhance the crystalline nature of the LDH and also increase the surface area of the LDH [24]. The schematic diagram for the synthesis of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs is shown in Scheme S1. The synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite is being explored for its electrocatalytic activity in OER.

The green pods of Cyamopsis tetragonoloba were purchased from local market in Dindigul. Aqueous ammonia and Nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) were purchased from Central Drug House (CDH), and Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL). Double distilled water (DD) was used to make all the electrolytic solutions. All the electrodes were purchased from Sinsil International Pvt. Ltd., Bengaluru, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The green pods of Cyamopsis tetragonoloba (50 g) were roughly cut into small pieces and ground well using the blender by adding double distilled water (50 mL). After grinding it, the extract was filtered using Whatmann 40 filter paper and used for making NDCDs further. In a 100 mL Teflon vessel, 56 mL of green extract (carbon source) and 4 mL of aqueous ammonia (nitrogen source) were taken and put into a stainless steel autoclave. This autoclave was kept inside the hot air oven for about 12 h at 180 °C. A dark brown colored liquid was obtained which was filtered using Whatman filter 40 paper and collected supernatant liquid was kept in refrigerator for further study.

The NDCDs (0.012 g) were sonicated for 1 h in 60 mL of DD water to disperse it. Further, Nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 0.2818 g), Ferric nitrate nonahydrate (0.1939 g) and NH4F (0.0148 g) were added to the dispersed solution. Then, the solution was stirred vigorously to make a clear solution. Eventually, a specific amount of Na2CO3 (1 M) and NaOH (1 M) were added to that stirring solution until it reached a pH of 10. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 120 °C for 15 h before cooling to room temperature. The final product was extracted by centrifugation and then washed many times with water and ethanol. The synthesized composite was denoted as the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs. For comparative study, similar procedure of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was followed for synthesizing NiFe-LDH without adding NDCDs.

The structural morphology of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was investigated by HR-TEM and SEM using FEI Technai G2 20 S-TWIN HR-TEM operating at 200 kV and VEGA3, TESCAN (Czech Republic). The composition of the elements of the prepared electrocatalyst was analyzed by a BRUKER Nano, GmbH, D-12489 Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy with an accelerating voltage of 30 keV. The XRD pattern of the synthesized catalyst was analysed by PANalytical X’Pert diffractometer equipment using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) operating at 45 kV and 40 mA. The Raman spectrum of the prepared NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was obtained using STR 500 nm focal length laser Raman spectrometer. The FT-IR (Jasco FT- IR 460 Plus) spectrum was recorded over the wave number range of 400–4000 cm−1 and XPS analyzer (PHI 5000 Versa Probe II) was used to characterize the functional groups and their electronic states present over the surface of NiFe-LDH@ NDCDs, respectively.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were used to examine the electrocatalytic activity of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs towards OER in 0.1 M KOH. The electrochemical experiments were carried out using CHI 760D electrochemical workstation with a standard three electrode system. The nickel foam coated with NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, silver/silver chloride, and platinum wire were used as the working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. 20 mg of synthesized NiFe-LDH/NDCDs were dispersed in a mixed solvent of 1.6 mL water and 0.4 mL ethanol and combined with 40 µL of 5% Nafion solution to make a homogenous ink for catalyst ink preparation. Then, the nickel foam of about 1 cm × 1 cm, was sonicated using acetone followed by 3 M hydrochloric acid for 20 mins. After sonication, the nickel foam was washed thoroughly with DD water - ethanol mixture and dried. Next, 30 µL of the prepared catalyst ink was uniformly loaded on the nickel foam through drop casting method. Potassium hydroxide (0.1 M) was used as the electrolyte in all the electrochemical experiments. The electrolytic solution was purged with high purity O2 for about 30 min before the electrochemical experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear sweep voltammetry and Tafel measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
The LSV measurements were performed from 0 to 1 V Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. All the potentials were presented in the Reversible Hydrogen Electrode (RHE) without iR drop compensation (Eq. (3)).
(3)
E
vs
.
RHE
=
E
vs
.
Ag
/
AgCl
+
E
Ag
/
AgCl
ref
+
0
.
0591 V
×
pH
E
Ag
/
AgCl
ref
=
0
.
1976
V

The Tafel plot was derived from LSV graphs and analysis was carried out based on following equation.
(4)
η =
a
+
b
log
j
(5)
η =
E
RHE
- 1.23 V
whereas η was the overpotential, j and b were current density and Tafel slope, respectively.
EIS studies were carried out in the range of 104 to 0.1 Hz with 5 mV of amplitude with different open circuit potentials and results were fitted with equivalent circuit.
Chronopotentiometry was used to evaluate the stability of synthesized material. The experiment was conducted at constant current density of 10 mA cm−2.

The double layer capacitance (Cdl) of the synthesized composite was determined from the cyclic voltammetry measurements. From the double layer capacitance, the electrochemically active surface areas (ECSA) were calculated. The cyclic voltammetric measurements were carried out at various scan rates from 10 to 100 mV/s in the non-faradic region (potential window from 0.15 to 0.30 VRHE), where the electrode was fully polarized. The Cdl was estimated from the slope of average current density vs scan rate from this measurement. The relationship between the Cdl and double layer charging current density (jdl) and scan rate (v) is shown in Eq. (6).
(6)
j
dl
= ν
C
dl
(7)
ECSA
=
C
dl
/
C
s
The ECSA was determined using the double layer capacitance (Cdl) and Cs from Eq. (7), where Cs is the material's specific capacitance in an identical electrolyte solution.

Using simple hydrothermal carbonization method, the NDCDs were prepared from the Cyamopsis tetragonoloba extract and it was visually noticed by the change of color from green to brown. The NiFe-LDH@NDCDs were synthesized by facile hydrothermal reaction using Nickel chloride, Ferric nitrate and synthesized NDCDs.

HR-TEM and SEM measurements were used to examine the structural morphology of the synthesized NDCDs and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite. The HR-TEM image of the synthesized NDCDs at 20 nm scale revealed the spherical shape and even distribution as shown in Fig. 1 a. Fig. 1b shows the bar graph for the size of NDCDs and the average size of the synthesized NDCDs was computed to be 4.3 nm [25]. The SAED pattern of synthesized NDCDs is shown in the Fig. 1c which clearly exposed a diffused ring pattern revealing the amorphous nature of the carbon dots [26].

SEM images of synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a&b). The images clearly showed the stacked nanosheets structure and confirmed the synthesis of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs [27]. The EDS spectrum of prepared NiFe-LDH@NDCDs is shown in Fig. 2c and the presence of nickel, iron, nitrogen, carbon, and oxygen in the composite could be confirmed, suggesting the development of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs [28]. The HR-TEM images of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs as shown in Fig. 2(d–f), clearly indicated the formation of the composite. Through the magnified image of the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, it is proved that the vertically aligned multilayer nano sheets could increase the surface area and create more active edge sites to elevate the electrocatalytic performance [29]. The inset magnified TEM image in Fig. 2f further revealed that the lattice fringe spacing of 0.27 nm successfully matched with the plane (012) of NiFe-LDH [30]. It is a significant confirmation towards the development of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs. The XRD pattern for the synthesized NDCDs and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs is shown in Fig. 3 . The diffraction broad pattern at 22.30 showed the C (002) plane of synthesized NDCDs. The d spacing value was computed using Bragg's equation to be 0.38 nm, which confirmed the amorphous nature of the NDCDs [31].

The synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs showed characteristic diffraction peaks 2θ at 11.50, 22.80, 34.40, and 61.30 corresponding to the NiFe LDH plane of (003), (006), (012), and (113), respectively. These values matched well with the standard data of NiFe LDH (JCPDS card 40-0215) [32–34]. The characteristic peak for carbon (22.30) was too weak to be observed due to the limited amount and lower diffraction intensity in the composite [5,48]. This is a significant evidence for the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite's formation.

XPS is an important tool to investigate the binding interaction and the surface oxidation state of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite. From the high resolution spectrum of C1s, as shown in the Fig. 4 a, the three peaks with the binding energy of 288.4 eV, 285.9 eV and 284.6 eV could be traced to the C=O, C=C and C–N groups, respectively [35,36] whereas from the high resolution spectrum of N1s level as shown in the Fig. 4b, the peaks at 400.8 eV and 399.6 eV indicated the presence of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively [37,38]. The deconvolution spectrum of O1s level shown in Fig. 4c showed peaks at 530.5, 531.2 and 532.5 eV which correspond to the metal–oxygen bond, C=O and C–O/N–O states, respectively [39]. The Fig. 4d showed the high resolution XPS spectrum of Ni 2p region. The two peaks positioned on the region of 855.9 eV and 873.6 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively along with two satellite peaks (861.7& 879.7 eV), which indicated the Ni2+ oxidation state of Nickel [40,41]. In the Fig. 4e, the peaks at 725.8 eV and 712.9 eV could be attributed to Fe 2p1/2, and Fe 2p3/2, respectively, confirming the existence of Fe3+ oxidation state [42,43]. Therefore, the existence of the carbon, oxygen and nitrogen species on the surface of the composite denoted the incorporation of NDCDs into NiFe-LDH.

The nature of functional groups present in the NDCDs and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs can be determined by the FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 5 ). The synthesized NDCDs showed IR bands at 3414, 2926, 1590, and 1115 cm−1 that indicated the presence of stretching frequencies of –OH/–NH, –CH, –C=O and –C–C– correspondingly [44,45]. The synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs showed bands at 3414, 2930, 1562, and 773 cm−1 attributed to the stretching frequencies of OH/–NH, –CH, –C=O and M−O respectively [46,47]. Compared with NDCDs, the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite showed a minor shift of 30 cm−1 owing to –C=O stretching of the composite. This important shift could be due to either electrostatic attraction among the NiFe LDH and NDCDs or the formation of M−O−C bond (M represents Fe or Ni). This information indicates that NDCDs not only have physical absorption with NiFe LDH, but also have further strong electrostatic interactions with the NiFe LDH [48].

Raman spectroscopy often plays a superior role in characterizing the carbon structured materials. The Raman spectrum of synthesized NDCDs (Fig. S2) exhibited two distinct peaks at 1366 cm−1 and 1602 cm−1 which specified the presence of D-band and G-band respectively. The G-band was associated to the vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon in the graphitic sheets, whereas the D-band was attributed to the sp3 defects in the graphitic sheets [49]. The ratio of D band and G band intensities (ID/IG) values was used to determine the graphitization of carbon nanostructured materials. The ID/IG value of NDCDs was around 0.50 which signified that the synthesized NDCDs had an adequate graphitic structure [50].

The optical properties of NDCDs were determined using UV–Visible spectroscopy. The UV–Visible spectra of synthesized NDCDs as shown in Fig. S3a, exhibited two characteristic shoulders at 270 nm and 330 nm which correspond to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of the C=C (sp2) or carbonyl and amine groups exist on the NDCDs, respectively [51].The photoluminescent excitation spectrum of synthesized NDCDs is shown in Fig. S3b. This spectrum exhibited three shoulders at 235 nm, 290 nm and 320 nm and this behavior implied that NDCDs surface had at least three types of excitation energy levels [52].

The fluorescence spectra of prepared NDCDs at different excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. S3. The Fig. S4a showed a gradual increase in the intensity of fluorescence from 270 nm to 320 nm and the Fig. S4b showed further increase in the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 400 nm with the decrease in the emission intensity. The maximum intensity of 400 nm was noticed with the excitation wavelength of 320 nm [53].

The OER performance of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was investigated in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte with three electrode system, in which NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF was used as the working electrode; Ag/AgCl and Pt wire were working as reference and counter electrode, correspondingly. Nickel foam (NF) was used as the working electrode since it has better active sites. Being a 3D electrode, NF had increased the rate of diffusion of gas and ease the penetration of the electrolye. To rate the OER performance of the working electrode, LSV was executed at the scan rate of 10 mV/s in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. For controlled experiments, similar measurements for RuO2 (state-of-the-art), NiFe-LDH, and Bare Nickel foam were also carried out and it is shown in Fig. 6 a.

It is evident from Fig. 6a that the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF exhibited higher electrocatalytic activity toward OER than Bare Ni foam, NDCDs, RuO2/NF, and NiFe-LDH/NF. Electro kinetic parameters such as over potential and onset potential were determined from LSV curves. The synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF required only 385 mV to attain 10 mA cm−2, which was 37 mV lesser than that of RuO2, which, clearly shows that the NiFe LDH activity on OER further enhanced by the addition of NDCDs, due to the exposure of more active sites. Also the strong interactions between NDCDs and NiFe LDH (this strong interaction confirmed by FT-IR) further felicitate the charge transfer and the catalytic activity enrichment. The oxidative peaks from 1.4 to 1.6 VRHE represented the oxidation of NiII to NiIII (which arose from the Ni foam and LDH) [54]. Fig. 6b showed the Tafel slope and overpotential values of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, RuO2, NiFe-LDH, NDCDs and Bare Ni foam. Ideally, the good OER catalyst must have lower over potential and high stability than the bare Ni foam [55]. The onset potentials of the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, RuO2, NiFe-LDH, NDCDs, and Bare Ni foam were 302, 348, 369, 385 and 415 mV, respectively. Muhammad Tahir et al. reported that the overpotential of the synthesized OER catalyst at 10 mA/cm2 was about 300–400 mV and suggested it to be an excellent catalyst [56]. The synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs activity was further compared with relevant research works and shown in Table S1 to depict the superiority of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs in OER. From the results and discussion, the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite had shown a greater OER performance than Bare Ni foam, NDCDs, RuO2, and NiFe-LDH.

Tafel slope is the pivotal factor to determine the electrocatalytic activity of the material and it is noticed that the materials with lower Tafel slope will have a faster OER kinetics [57]. The Tafel slopes of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, RuO2, NiFe-LDH, NDCDs, and Bare Ni foams are shown in Fig. 7 . The calculated linear part of Tafel slope values for NiFe-LDH@NDCDs, RuO2, NiFe-LDH, NDCDs, and Bare Ni foam were 93, 128, 132, 152, and 170 mV/dec, respectively. The lower Tafel slope of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs indicated the faster kinetic rate of OER. Based on these results, we can conclude that NiFe LDH@NDCDs composite not only improved the catalytic OER activity, but also sped up the charge transfer process.

Further, the longevity and durability of the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF composite was analysed using chronopotentiometry (Fig. 8 ). After running at constant current density of 10 mA/cm2 for about 6 h, a slight change (0.3% loss) was observed from 1.620 to 1.617 V vs VRHE. This showed that the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF possessed good stability and high activity in basic medium [58].

EIS is a vital technique to explore the coating effect and kinetic properties of the synthesized material [59]. The impedance study was executed for bare Ni foam, NiFe LDH/NF and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF at their corresponding open circuit potentials of 0.288 V, 0.346 V and 0.315 V vs Ag/AgCl. Fig. 9 shows the Nyquist plots for the bare Ni foam, synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF and NiFe LDH (inset) and the results were fitted with equivalent circuits (Fig. S5). The Rs (solution resistance) values of bare Ni foam, NiFe LDH and NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF were 26.89, 19.89 and 24.65 Ω/cm2, respectively and the Rs values indicated the environmental similarity of the electrolyte solution. In fitted circuit, the obtained total resistance (R1 (coating resistance) + R2 (charge transfer resistance)) of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF was 63.85 Ω/cm2 which was lower than the total resistance of NiFe LDH/NF (82.58 Ω/cm2) and also the diffusion resistance (R1) (123.8 Ω/cm2) of bare Ni foam. The smaller total resistance value of NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF evidenced the faster electron transfer from NDCDs to NiFe LDH on surface of the composite which additionally enhanced the OER activities [60].
In terms of ECSA, it is widely known that electrocatalytic activity of materials is mainly dependent on the Cdl [61].

The ECSA of synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was evaluated using cyclic voltammetry (potential window 0.15–0.30 VRHE) at several scan rates (10, 20, 25, 30, 50, 75, 100 mV/s). Fig. 10 a &b show the CV graphs at various scan rates in 0.1 M KOH, and linear fit of current density and scan rates, respectively. From the results, the Cdl of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite was calculated to be 0.0031 mF/cm2. Moreover, the ECSA of synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was determined from the Eq. (7) ECSA = Cdl/Cs. The specific capacitance of the synthesized catalyst (Cs) was 40 µF/cm2 in the 0.1 M KOH [62,63]. The calculated ECSA for the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was 0.0775 cm2, which decreased the charge transfer resistance and enhanced the OER kinetics.

The proposed mechanism for OER activity of the synthesized NiFe LDH@NDCDs is given in the Fig. 11 based on the results. Though NiFe LDH plays a major role in getting high OER activity, the enhancement of the OER activity is achieved by the robust interactions of NDCDs and NiFe LDH. Hence, NDCDs have vast properties like smaller size, fast electron transfer, and good conducting nature. Particularly, the smaller size of NDCDs (4.3 nm), brings a larger specific surface area to permit the oxygen evolution through faster electron transfer from NDCDs to NiFe LDH. Further, the functional groups (like carboxyl C=O) on the NDCDs surface lead to the formation of new covalent bond of C–O–Fe or C–O–Ni assisting the formation of NiFe LDH@NDCDs composite easier due to the strong electrostatic interaction among NDCDs and NiFe LDH. However, the improvement of OER activity leads to synergistic effect enabled by proper integration of NiFe LDH with NDCDs surface functional groups (C=O). From the outcome, it is established that the NDCDs could greatly boost the electrocatalytic OER of NiFe-LDH caused by electrostatic interaction between NiFe LDH and NDCDs.

To sum up, a novel NiFe-LDH@NDCDs composite with stacked nanosheets structure has been synthesized effectively using simple solvothermal method. The XPS and FT-IR studies explored the active functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups (pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen) which were functionalized on the NiFe-LDH@NDCDs. The electrocatalytic OER was evaluated in 0.1 M KOH and the overpotential of prepared NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF was 0.385 VRHE, which was lesser than that of the NiFe-LDH/NF (0.425 VRHE). The Tafel slopes of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs was about 93 mV/dec, which was lower than the NiFe-LDH (132 mV/dec), RuO2 (128 mV/dec), NDCDs (152 mV/dec) and Bare Ni foam (170 mV/dec). From the impedance results, the total resistance value of the synthesized NiFe-LDH@NDCDs/NF was lower than the bare Ni foam. Hence the electrochemical results showed that the Cyamopsis tetragonoloba derived NDCDs supported NiFe-LDH composite (NiFe-LDH@NDCDs) exhibited significant electrocatalytic activity towards OER. The synthesized elctrocatalyst can be used for the oxygen production for the future fuel requirement.

Pitchai Chandrasekaran: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft. Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison: Validation, Visualization. Mathur Gopalakrishnan Sethuraman: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) for the financial support for this work through the NET-JRF Award (Award No: 09/715(0020)/2019-EMR-I dated on 02/07/2019).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2022.123947.
